topic: Two strangers realize they've been dating the same person
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in Elara’s mouth. She watched Mark across the crowded gallery, his easy smile directed entirely at the woman beside him. A slow, tight knot formed in her stomach, a sensation she immediately tried to physically smooth out by gripping her purse strap until her knuckles were white. It was absurd. Of course, it was absurd. She’d spent the last three months meticulously cataloging every shared glance, every inside joke, every seemingly unique detail of their connection, only to realize now that the entire edifice was built on a shared foundation she hadn't even been invited to inspect. When Mark finally turned, his eyes met hers, and for a fleeting second, a flicker of something—recognition mixed with a strange, cold triumph—crossed his face before he smoothly diverted his attention back to her. Elara felt a sharp, unpleasant tightening behind her ribs, a desire to dismantle the whole arrangement just to see the structure crumble.

[story 2]

"That’s a terrible shade of blue," Liam remarked, leaning against the counter, his voice laced with an easy familiarity that made Elara’s jaw clench. She forced a polite laugh, the sound brittle and thin. She’d been waiting for this moment, for the universe to deliver the confirmation of her own meticulous observation. He was wearing the exact same slightly crooked silver ring she’d seen him wear in that obscure documentary footage from six months ago. The air around them seemed to thicken, charged with an unwelcome awareness. When he finally looked directly at her, his expression was one of mild surprise, quickly masked by a practiced nonchalance. Elara felt a sudden, hot flush rise up her neck, and she deliberately focused on adjusting her scarf, her movements stiff and overly deliberate. She wanted to make him feel the sudden, jarring dissonance of her realization, to see the small crack appear in his composure.

[story 3]

Maya traced the rim of her glass, the condensation cool against her fingertips. Across the dimly lit bar, David was laughing, a sound she knew intimately, a sound that had soundtracked countless evenings she’d thought were uniquely hers. A wave of intense irritation washed over her, so potent it made her want to push the glass away. She noted the way he tilted his head when he spoke, the almost imperceptible shift in his posture that mirrored a gesture she’d only ever witnessed in passing. When their eyes locked—a brief, accidental collision—Maya held the gaze a fraction too long, her expression carefully neutral, bordering on challenging. She wanted him to sense the sudden, profound shift in her internal landscape, the realization that she was privy to a secret he thought was safely buried, and she intended to make him feel the weight of that knowledge.
